summary: Jeff Atwood presents a UI design principle that goes beyond simply making software easy to use. He argues that good interface design involves deliberately making desirable actions easy while making undesirable or risky actions more difficult. Drawing an analogy to animal training techniques, he frames UI design as a form of behavioral conditioning—rewarding good behavior with simplicity and discouraging unwanted behavior through intentional friction.
key_points: Good UI design makes the right actions easy and wrong actions awkward—not uniformly simple
Risky or dangerous operations should deliberately require more steps
UI design is essentially user training through behavioral rewards and friction
Making features hard to use is a legitimate design technique when used judiciously
The 'pit of success' concept means users should naturally fall into correct usage patterns
money_quotes: Make the right thing easy to do and the wrong thing awkward to do.
Sometimes there are things you don't want users to do. In those cases, you actually want your application, or at least certain areas of it, to be harder to use.
You don't get a sea lion to balance a ball on the end of its nose by nagging.
When you make features easy to use, you are rewarding user behavior you like.
When you intentionally choose not to make a feature easy to use, you are effectively ignoring user behavior you don't like.
If you aren't taking advantage of both techniques in your user interface—rewarding with simplicity, and judiciously ignoring with complexity where necessary—you aren't properly training your users.
key_insight: Effective UI design is behavioral training: reward desired actions with simplicity and discourage risky ones with deliberate friction.
